title: React
purpose: Build component-driven interfaces with predictable state, accessible interactions and efficient rendering.
outcomes: Explain React concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important React features
Implement, test and debug practical React solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of React before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: JSX, components and props
topics.definition: React renders a component tree as a function of props and state, then reconciles the next tree with the previous one to update the host UI.
topics.whyItMatters: It makes interactive UI predictable by describing desired output for each state instead of manually synchronizing many DOM mutations.
whenToUse: Represent loading, empty, success and error states explicitly
whenNotToUse: Using an effect to derive a value that can be calculated during render
Putting every state value in global context
Adding memoization before finding an actual render cost
internalWorking: A state update schedules rendering
React calls components to produce a new element tree
Reconciliation uses element type and keys to preserve or replace state
The commit phase applies DOM changes; effects run to synchronize external systems
tradeoffs: Declarative state improves consistency but rerenders require performance awareness
Local state is simple while shared state needs clear ownership
Abstraction through hooks helps reuse but can hide lifecycle behavior
commonMistakes: Mutating state directly
Using array indexes as keys for reorderable data
Omitting effect dependencies to suppress reruns
topics.practicalExample: Keep selected roadmap and search text as local state, derive the filtered list during render, and use an effect only if an external browser or network system must be synchronized.

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of JSX, components and props.
interviewQuestions.answer: React treats UI as a function of props and state. Updates schedule a render, reconciliation compares element types and keys, and the commit phase applies changes. Effects are for external synchronization, not ordinary derivation, and state should live at the lowest owner shared by its consumers.

topics.title: Forms and lifting state
topics.definition: Semantic HTML uses elements according to their meaning so browsers and assistive technology can provide correct structure and interaction behavior.
topics.whyItMatters: A visual interface alone does not communicate roles, names, relationships or keyboard behavior to every user and device.
whenToUse: Use headings and landmarks to describe document structure
Use native buttons, links, inputs and labels for interaction
Use ARIA only to fill a semantic gap that native HTML cannot cover
whenNotToUse: Clickable div elements
ARIA roles that conflict with native behavior
Placeholder text as the only form label
internalWorking: The browser parses markup into the DOM
Semantics contribute to the accessibility tree
Native controls expose keyboard, focus and state behavior
Labels create an accessible name used by assistive technology
tradeoffs: Native controls are robust but may require deliberate styling
Custom widgets offer unique behavior but carry full accessibility responsibility
Extra markup can aid structure but meaningless wrappers add noise
commonMistakes: Skipping heading levels for appearance
Using a button for navigation or a link for an action
Adding tabindex to every element
topics.practicalExample: Use a form with an associated email label, native validation and a submit button; announce server errors beside the field and move focus only when necessary.

topics.title: Routing and data fetching
topics.definition: A promise represents the eventual completion or failure of asynchronous work. async/await provides sequential syntax while preserving promise-based execution.
topics.whyItMatters: Network and timer operations finish later; promises provide one composable value for success, failure and chaining.
whenToUse: Coordinate API requests and other non-blocking operations
Run independent operations concurrently with an explicit aggregate
whenNotToUse: Awaiting independent operations one by one
Creating a promise around an already promise-based API
Ignoring rejection or component cleanup
internalWorking: Calling an async function immediately returns a promise
Await suspends that function, not the JavaScript thread
Promise reactions enter the microtask queue
The event loop runs microtasks after the current stack before the next task
tradeoffs: Readable sequential syntax versus hidden concurrency if awaits are misplaced
Promise.all is fast-fail and concurrent but needs a partial-failure policy
Cancellation is cooperative rather than automatic
commonMistakes: Forgetting to return or await a promise
Using array forEach with async callbacks
Updating UI from a stale request
topics.practicalExample: Load profile and roadmap concurrently with Promise.all, pass one AbortSignal to both, and show a recoverable error if either required request fails.

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of Stable keys identify data.
interviewQuestions.answer: React treats UI as a function of props and state. Updates schedule a render, reconciliation compares element types and keys, and the commit phase applies changes. Effects are for external synchronization, not ordinary derivation, and state should live at the lowest owner shared by its consumers.
